---
title: "12.1 Starting Rust for scientific computing"
---

## Explanation

Rust can be the first implementation language for a scientific-computing
project. An AI agent can help with syntax, Cargo setup, compiler errors, tests,
and refactoring. The human role is to keep the specification, validation data,
and scientific meaning clear.

The important question is not which language comes first. The important
question is whether the project has a clear specification, small tested
functions, reference data, and a validation workflow. With those in place, Rust
is a reasonable language for the first implementation.

Rust is useful for scientific software because it is different in ways that
matter for larger practical code:

- Rust is statically typed. Many errors are caught before the program runs.
- Rust has a strong package and build system through Cargo.
- Rust has no garbage collector. Memory management is controlled through ownership and borrowing.
- Safe Rust rejects uncontrolled mutable aliasing. You can have many immutable
  references, or one mutable reference, but not arbitrary mutation through
  multiple aliases at the same time.
- Compiler errors are often useful feedback when an AI agent generates code.
- Rust is used beyond research code. For example, [Linux kernel
  documentation](https://www.kernel.org/doc/html/v6.17/rust/index.html) says
  Rust support was merged into the mainline kernel in version 6.1, although it
  still describes this support as development or experimental for some uses.

The goal is not to memorize Rust syntax before writing anything. The goal is to
understand generated Rust code well enough to review data structures, ownership,
tests, benchmarks, and scientific claims.

For an overview, start from the official Rust learning page: [Learn
Rust](https://rust-lang.org/learn/).

## Things to look up

- Rust programming language
- Cargo
- Ownership
- Borrowing

## Exercise

Open the official [Learn Rust](https://rust-lang.org/learn/) page. Read the
overview and follow the beginner path far enough to recognize the words
ownership, borrowing, Cargo, and crate.

Do not try to write a full Rust program yet. Write down three Rust terms that
look important but unfamiliar. Ask an AI agent to explain each term using a
small numerical function that takes a `Vec<f64>` or slice. Then continue to the
next page and use those terms while reading Rust function signatures.

## Notes for the exercise

- Do not try to learn the whole language before writing small functions.
- Treat compiler errors as review feedback, not as a failure of the workflow.
- For first contact with Rust, use official learning material rather than
  random snippets.
